mnemonics.title: Waterfall Phases Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: R-D-I-T-D-M: 'Ravi Designed IT During Marriage' - Requirements, Design, Implementation, Testing, Deployment, Maintenance

mnemonics.title: Waterfall Disadvantages Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: L-I-F-E: Late testing, Inflexible to changes, Few working deliverables, Escalating cost of changes

mnemonics.title: Waterfall Advantages Mnemonic
mnemonics.content: S-D-P: Simple, Documented, Predictable - the three pillars of traditional project success

acronyms.expansion: Software Requirements Review - gate between Requirements and Design
acronyms.usage: Formal sign-off on requirements document before design begins. Must be approved by stakeholders and technical team.

acronyms.expansion: System Design Review - gate between Design and Implementation
acronyms.usage: Validates architectural decisions, technology choices, and design documents against requirements.

acronyms.expansion: Preliminary Design Review - early design gate
acronyms.usage: Reviews high-level design before detailed design begins. Identifies major technical risks early.

acronyms.expansion: Test Readiness Review - gate before Testing phase
acronyms.usage: Confirms implementation is complete and test environment is ready before formal testing begins.

acronyms.expansion: Change Request Analysis - formal process for requirements changes
acronyms.usage: Evaluates impact, cost, and schedule implications of any change. Required before approval in Waterfall.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine water flowing down a staircase - each step represents a phase. Water (project progress) cannot flow backward naturally. Only at designated overflow points (review gates) can flow redirect. The waterfall gets 'wider' at top (more options) and 'narrower' at bottom (committed decisions).

Each phase produces a 'box' of documents. As you go down, boxes stack. Testing phase opens all boxes to verify. If one box has issues, all boxes below are affected - this visualizes the cascade effect of errors.

Y-axis = Cost to fix, X-axis = Project Timeline. Waterfall shows exponential curve - fixing requirements error at end costs 100x vs catching it during requirements. This is why stable requirements are critical for Waterfall success.

analogies.analogy: Waterfall is like building a skyscraper: foundation (requirements) must be perfect, then structural framework (design), then walls/windows (implementation), then inspection (testing), then occupancy (deployment). You can't add floors after inspection reveals foundation flaws - would require demolition.

Each phase gate is like airport security: you must pass through each checkpoint before proceeding to next area. Missing documents (deliverables) means going back to gather them. You can't board the plane (reach deployment) without clearing all security stages.

Waterfall's testing phase is like a university where students never receive feedback until the final exam. If they fail, they must restart the entire course. Continuous assessment (iterative testing) would catch problems earlier and allow remediation.
